words.shortDefinition: An agile framework for developing, delivering, and sustaining complex products.
words.longDefinition: Scrum emphasizes iterative progress through short, fixed-length 'sprints'. It relies on transparency, inspection, and adaptation, with specific roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master) and events (Daily Stand-up, Retrospective).
words.techContext: Our engineering team uses Scrum to deliver new software increments every two weeks.
words.exampleSentence: During the sprint planning, we realized that the Scrum framework would help us better manage the unpredictable nature of our new research project.
words.etymology: from the game of rugby, where a 'scrum' is a way of restarting play involving players packing closely together.

words.shortDefinition: A visual system for managing work as it moves through a process.
words.longDefinition: Focuses on continuous delivery and just-in-time production. Unlike Scrum, it has no fixed-length iterations. It uses a board with columns to represent stages of work and 'WIP limits' to prevent bottlenecks.
words.techContext: Our support team uses a Kanban board to manage incoming bug reports and ensure they are addressed in order of priority.
words.exampleSentence: Switching to Kanban allowed us to respond to urgent customer requests more quickly than our previous sprint-based model.

words.shortDefinition: A unit of measure for expressing an estimate of the overall effort required to implement a user story.
words.longDefinition: Instead of estimating in hours, teams use story points to represent the relative complexity, risk, and effort of a task. Common scales include the Fibonacci sequence (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13).
words.techContext: We assigned 5 story points to the new authentication feature because of the complexity involved in supporting multi-factor auth.
words.exampleSentence: Estimating in story points helps our team focus on the 'size' of the work rather than the exact number of hours it will take.

words.shortDefinition: The amount of work a team can complete during a single sprint.
words.longDefinition: Calculated by summing the story points of all user stories that were completed (according to the Definition of Done) during an iteration. It is used to help the team predict how much work they can handle in future sprints.
words.techContext: Our average velocity is 45 points per sprint, which helps our Product Owner plan our roadmap for the next quarter.
words.exampleSentence: The team's velocity dropped this week because two developers were out on vacation.

words.shortDefinition: A graphical representation of work left to do versus time.
words.longDefinition: The vertical axis usually represents the amount of work (in story points or tasks), and the horizontal axis represents time. It is used to predict when all of the work will be completed and to see if the team is on track for the sprint goal.
words.techContext: Looking at the burndown chart, we realized we were lagging behind and decided to move one low-priority task back to the backlog.
words.exampleSentence: The burndown chart showed a steep drop on Thursday as we finished the complex database migration.

words.shortDefinition: A fixed period of time during which specific work has to be completed and made ready for review.
words.longDefinition: The basic unit of development in Scrum. Sprints are typically 1 to 4 weeks long. Once a sprint begins, its duration is fixed and cannot be shortened or lengthened.
words.techContext: We are currently in the middle of Sprint 24, focusing on improving the mobile app's performance.
words.exampleSentence: The goal for this sprint is to integrate the new payment gateway API.
words.etymology: from Old Norse spretta ('to jump up'), used in athletics for short-distance races.

words.shortDefinition: An ordered list of everything that is known to be needed in the product.
words.longDefinition: The single source of truth for requirements for any changes to be made to the product. It is dynamic and constantly evolves as the product and the environment in which it will be used evolves.
words.techContext: I spent the afternoon cleaning up the product backlog and closing old feature requests that are no longer relevant.
words.exampleSentence: The top items on the product backlog are always the most refined and ready for development.

words.shortDefinition: A short daily meeting for the development team to synchronize activities and plan for the next 24 hours.
words.longDefinition: Typically limited to 15 minutes. Each team member answers three questions: What did I do yesterday? What will I do today? Are there any blockers? It is usually held standing up to keep it brief.
words.techContext: At the daily stand-up, I mentioned that I'm blocked by a delay in the API documentation from the other team.
words.exampleSentence: The daily stand-up is our chance to quickly identify any issues that might prevent us from reaching our sprint goal.
words.etymology: Descriptive of the meeting's daily frequency and standing posture.

words.shortDefinition: A meeting held at the end of a sprint to discuss what went well and what could be improved.
words.longDefinition: The 'Retro' is an opportunity for the Scrum Team to inspect itself and create a plan for improvements to be enacted during the next Sprint. It focuses on the team's processes, tools, and relationships.
words.techContext: In our last retrospective, we decided to try using a new automated testing tool to reduce manual QA time.
words.exampleSentence: We identified a communication gap during the retrospective and agreed to hold more frequent design syncs.

words.shortDefinition: An informal, natural language description of one or more features of a software system.
words.longDefinition: Typically written from the perspective of an end user or user of a system. The common format is: 'As a [user], I want [action], so that [benefit]'.
words.techContext: I added a user story for the search feature so the developers understand why the 'sort by price' filter is important for our customers.
words.exampleSentence: A good user story is small enough to be completed within a single sprint.

codeExample.code:
As a buyer,
I want to see my order history,
So that I can easily re-order items I've bought before.

words.shortDefinition: The conditions that a software product must satisfy to be accepted by a user or customer.
words.longDefinition: Detailed requirements that define exactly what a user story needs to do to be considered complete and working correctly. They provide a clear 'pass/fail' test for developers and QA engineers.
words.techContext: The user story is almost finished, but I still need to satisfy the acceptance criteria regarding error handling for invalid credit cards.
words.exampleSentence: We wrote the acceptance criteria together during the refinement session to ensure everyone was on the same page.
words.etymology: from Latin acceptare ('to take, receive') + criteria ('standards').

words.shortDefinition: A shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete.
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as DoD. It's a checklist of all the things a user story must have before it can be considered finished (e.g., code reviewed, unit tests passed, documentation updated, deployed to staging).
words.techContext: According to our Definition of Done, the feature isn't finished until the security scan has come back clean.
words.exampleSentence: We updated our Definition of Done to include a requirement for cross-browser testing on Safari.

words.shortDefinition: A large body of work that can be broken down into a number of smaller stories.
words.longDefinition: Epics are used to organize tasks and represent larger goals. They often span multiple sprints and are used to track the progress of high-level initiatives.
words.techContext: We created an epic for the 'Mobile App Redesign' which contains over twenty individual user stories.
words.exampleSentence: The epic for the new analytics dashboard is finally 80% complete after three months of development.
words.etymology: from Greek epikos, traditionally referring to a long poem about heroic deeds.

words.shortDefinition: The person responsible for promoting and supporting Scrum.
words.longDefinition: A servant-leader for the Scrum Team. They help everyone understand Scrum theory, practices, and rules. They also help the team by removing 'impediments' (blockers) to the team's progress.
words.techContext: The Scrum Master facilitated the meeting and helped us resolve a conflict regarding how to estimate the new technical debt tasks.
words.exampleSentence: Our Scrum Master is great at shielding the developers from outside distractions during the sprint.

words.shortDefinition: The person responsible for maximizing the value of the product and the work of the development team.
words.longDefinition: The PO is the sole person responsible for managing the Product Backlog. They represent the business and the customers, deciding which features are most important and ensuring the team is working on the right things.
words.techContext: I met with the Product Owner to discuss the priority of the new API features for our enterprise clients.
words.exampleSentence: The Product Owner decided to move the login redesign to the next sprint based on user feedback.

words.shortDefinition: Work In Progress limit; a constraint on the number of items that can be in a given state of a workflow.
words.longDefinition: A key concept in Kanban. By limiting how many tasks can be 'In Progress' at once, teams can identify bottlenecks, improve focus, and reduce the time it takes for a single task to finish (cycle time).
words.techContext: We hit our WIP limit in the 'Testing' column, so two developers stopped coding and started helping the QA team clear the backlog.
words.exampleSentence: Setting a WIP limit of 3 on our development column prevented us from starting too many things at once.

words.shortDefinition: The time it takes to complete a task from the moment development starts until it is finished.
words.longDefinition: Measures the efficiency of the team's internal process. Lower cycle times indicate a faster and more predictable development flow.
words.techContext: By automating our deployment process, we reduced our average cycle time for bug fixes from 4 days to 6 hours.
words.exampleSentence: We track cycle time for each user story to identify which parts of our workflow are causing delays.

words.shortDefinition: The total time from when a request is made until it is fulfilled and delivered to the customer.
words.longDefinition: Includes the time spent waiting in the backlog plus the actual development time (cycle time). It measures the responsiveness of the entire organization to customer needs.
words.techContext: Our customers are complaining that lead time for new features is over six months, so we need to improve our backlog refinement process.
words.exampleSentence: Reducing lead time is our top priority for improving customer satisfaction this year.

words.shortDefinition: The implied cost of additional rework caused by choosing an easy solution now instead of a better approach that would take longer.
words.longDefinition: Just like financial debt, technical debt accrues interest in the form of extra effort needed in the future. If not managed, it can make it harder and slower to add new features or fix bugs over time.
words.techContext: We decided to take on some technical debt by skipping the unit tests for this prototype, but we've added a task to the backlog to fix it next sprint.
words.exampleSentence: The team spent the entire sprint just paying down technical debt that had built up over the last year.
words.etymology: Metaphor comparing software maintenance to financial 'debt'.

words.shortDefinition: A sequential development process where each phase must be completed before the next one begins.
words.longDefinition: A traditional, non-agile project management methodology. Phases typically include Requirements, Design, Implementation, Verification, and Maintenance. It is often criticized for being too rigid and failing to handle changing requirements.
words.techContext: We moved away from the Waterfall model because it took us too long to get feedback from real users.
words.exampleSentence: In a Waterfall project, if you discover a requirement error during the testing phase, it is extremely expensive to fix.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a process that flows in one direction, like water over a cliff.

words.shortDefinition: A consensus-based technique for estimating the relative size of software development tasks.
words.longDefinition: Team members use cards with Fibonacci numbers to vote on the complexity of a user story. If there is a disagreement, the team discusses the task until they reach a common understanding and a consensus estimate.
words.techContext: We used Planning Poker this morning to estimate the remaining stories in the backlog, and it helped us uncover some hidden complexities in the UI design.
words.exampleSentence: Planning poker prevents 'anchor bias' where everyone just agrees with the first person who speaks.

words.shortDefinition: A business-readable, domain-specific language used for defining test cases.
words.longDefinition: Uses a 'Given-When-Then' structure to describe the behavior of a feature. It allows non-technical stakeholders to write and understand automated test scenarios. It is the standard language for Cucumber and other BDD tools.
words.techContext: I wrote a Gherkin script to define the acceptance criteria for the new password reset flow.
words.exampleSentence: By writing our requirements in Gherkin, we ensure that the developers, testers, and product owners all have the same understanding of the feature.
words.etymology: Named after the 'gherkin' (pickled cucumber), following the Cucumber testing tool theme.

codeExample.code:
Given I am on the login page
When I enter my credentials
Then I should see my dashboard

words.shortDefinition: The set of product backlog items selected for the sprint, plus a plan for delivering them.
words.longDefinition: While the Product Backlog is for the whole project, the Sprint Backlog is just for the current iteration. It is managed by the development team and shows all the work they have committed to finishing by the end of the sprint.
words.techContext: I moved the API integration task into the Sprint Backlog since we've already started working on it.
words.exampleSentence: The Sprint Backlog is updated throughout the sprint as the team completes tasks and discovers new work.

words.shortDefinition: Cumulative Flow Diagram; an area chart that shows the status of work items for a given time period.
words.longDefinition: Shows how much work is in each stage of the process (e.g., Backlog, In Progress, Done) over time. It helps identify bottlenecks and visualize the stability of the team's workflow.
words.techContext: The CFD showed that the 'Review' stage was growing wider, indicating that our code reviews are taking longer than they used to.
words.exampleSentence: A healthy CFD should show smooth, parallel bands that all move upwards at a consistent rate.

words.shortDefinition: The process of adding detail, estimates, and order to items in the product backlog.
words.longDefinition: Also known as 'Backlog Grooming'. It's an ongoing activity where the Product Owner and the team collaborate to ensure that upcoming user stories are clear, correctly sized, and ready for development.
words.techContext: We spent an hour in backlog refinement to break down the large search epic into five smaller, manageable user stories.
words.exampleSentence: Regular refinement sessions make sprint planning much faster and more efficient.
